content: The area bounded by the coordinate axes and the curve $$\sqrt x  + \sqrt y  = 1$$ is
explanation:
Image

$$\therefore$$ Required area

$$ = \int_0^1 {{{(1 - \sqrt x )}^2}dx} $$

$$ = \int_0^1 {(1 + x - 2\sqrt x )dx} $$

$$ = \left[ {x + {{{x^2}} \over 2} - 2.{{{x^{3/2}}} \over {3/2}}} \right]_0^1$$

$$ = \left[ {1 + {1 \over 2} - 2 \times {2 \over 3}(1)} \right] - 0$$

$$ = 1 + {1 \over 2} - {4 \over 3} = {{6 + 3 - 8} \over 6}$$

$$ = {1 \over 6}$$ sq. unit

content: What is the area bounded by the curves | y | = 1 $$-$$ x<sup>2</sup> ?
explanation:
We have,

| y | = 1 $$-$$ x2

We have to find area between the curves

y = 1 $$-$$ x2 and y = x2 $$-$$ 1

Image

$$\therefore$$ Required area

$$ = 2\int_{ - 1}^1 {(1 - {x^2})dx} $$

$$ = 2\left[ {x - {{{x^3}} \over 3}} \right]_{ - 1}^1$$

$$ = 2\left[ {\left( {1 - {1 \over 3}} \right) - \left( { - 1 + {1 \over 3}} \right)} \right]$$

$$ = 2\left[ {{2 \over 3} + {2 \over 3}} \right] = {8 \over 3}$$ sq. units

direction:
Read the following information carefully and answer the questions given below.

Consider the function

f(x) = | x $$-$$ 1 | + x2, where x $$\in $$ R.
content: What is the area of the region bounded by X-axis, the curve y = f(x) and the two ordinates $$x = {1 \over 2}$$ and x = 1 ?
explanation:
f(x) = | x $$-$$ 1 | + x2

When $${1 \over 2}$$ < x < 1

f(x) = x2 $$-$$ x + 1

$$\therefore$$ Area of required region will be

Image

$$\int_{1/2}^1 {f(x)dx = \int_{1/2}^1 {({x^2} - x + 1)dx} } $$

$$ = \left[ {{{{x^3}} \over 3} - {{{x^2}} \over 2} + x} \right]_{1/2}^1$$

$$ = \left[ {{{{{(1)}^3}} \over 3} - {{{{(1)}^2}} \over 2} + (1)} \right] - \left[ {{{{{\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)}^3}} \over 3} - {{{{\left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)}^2}} \over 2} + {1 \over 2}} \right]$$

$$ = \left( {{1 \over 3} - {1 \over 2} + 1} \right) - \left( {{1 \over {24}} - {1 \over 8} + {1 \over 2}} \right)$$

$$ = \left( {{{2 - 3 + 6} \over 6}} \right) - \left( {{{1 - 3 + 12} \over {24}}} \right)$$

$$ = {5 \over 6} - {{10} \over {24}} = {{20 - 10} \over {24}} = {{10} \over {24}} = {5 \over {12}}$$ sq. unit

direction:
Read the following information carefully and answer the questions given below.

Consider the function

f(x) = | x $$-$$ 1 | + x2, where x $$\in $$ R.
content: What is the area of the region bounded by X-axis, the curve y = f(x) and the two ordinates x = 1 and x = $${3 \over 2}$$ ?
explanation:
f(x) = | x $$-$$ 1 | + x2

When 1 < x < $${3 \over {2}}$$, f(x) = x2 + x $$-$$ 1

Image

$$\therefore$$ Area of required region will be

$$\int_1^{{3 \over 2}} {f(x)dx = \int_1^{{3 \over 2}} {({x^2} + x - 1)dx} } $$

$$ = \left[ {{{{x^3}} \over 3} + {{{x^2}} \over 2} - x} \right]_1^{3/2}$$

$$ = \left[ {{{{{\left( {{3 \over 2}} \right)}^3}} \over 3} + {{{{\left( {{3 \over 2}} \right)}^2}} \over 2} - {3 \over 2}} \right] - \left[ {{{{{(1)}^3}} \over 3} + {{{{(1)}^2}} \over 2} - 1} \right]$$

$$ = \left( {{{27} \over {24}} + {9 \over 8} - {3 \over 2}} \right) - \left( {{1 \over 3} + {1 \over 2} - 1} \right)$$

$$ = \left( {{{27 + 27 - 36} \over {24}}} \right) - \left( {{{2 + 3 - 6} \over 6}} \right)$$

$$ = {{18} \over {24}} + {1 \over 6} = {{18 + 4} \over {24}} = {{22} \over {24}} = {{11} \over {12}}$$ sq. unit

content: The area bounded by the curve | x | + | y | = 1
explanation:
We have, | x | + | y | = 1

$$ = \left\{ \matrix{ x + y = 1,\,I\,quadrant \hfill \cr - x + y = 1,\,II\,quadrant \hfill \cr - x - y = 1,\,III\,quadrant \hfill \cr x - y = 1,\,IV\,quadrant \hfill \cr} \right.$$

Image

$$\therefore$$ Required area = 4 $$\times$$ area ($$\Delta$$OAB)

$$ = 4\int_0^1 {(1 - x)dx = 4\left[ {x - {{{x^2}} \over 2}} \right]_0^1} $$

$$ = 4\left[ {\left( {1 - {1 \over 2}} \right) - (0 - 0)} \right]$$

$$ = 4 \times {1 \over 2} = 2$$ sq. units

content: What is the are of the region bounded by the parabolas y<sup>2</sup> = 6(x $$-$$ 1) and y<sup>2</sup> = 3x ?
explanation:
Given,

y2 = 6(x $$-$$ 1) ...... (i)

and y2 = 3x

On solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

x = 2 and y = $$\pm$$ $${\sqrt 6 }$$

Image

$$\therefore$$ Required area = $$\int\limits_{ - \sqrt 6 }^{\sqrt 6 } {\left( {1 + {{{y^2}} \over 6} - {{{y^2}} \over 3}} \right)dy} $$

$$ = 2\int\limits_0^{\sqrt 6 } {\left( {1 - {{{y^2}} \over 6}} \right)dy = 2\left[ {y - {{{y^3}} \over {18}}} \right]_0^{\sqrt 6 }} $$

$$ = 2 \times \left[ {{{18y - {y^3}} \over {18}}} \right]_0^{\sqrt 6 }$$

$$ = 2 \times \left[ {{{18\sqrt 6 - 6\sqrt 6 } \over {18}}} \right]$$

$$ = {{12\sqrt 6 } \over 9} = {{4\sqrt 6 } \over 3}$$

content: What is the area of the region enclosed between the curve y<sup>2</sup> = 2x and the straight line y = x?
explanation:
Equations of curves,

y2 = 2x ....... (i)

and y = x .... (ii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii),

y2 = 2y

$$\Rightarrow$$ y(y $$-$$ 2) = 0

$$\therefore$$ y = 0, 2

$$\therefore$$ Required area

$$ = \int_0^2 {\left( {{{{y^2}} \over 2} - y} \right)} \,dy$$

$$ = \left[ {{1 \over 2}.{{{y^3}} \over 3} - {{{y^2}} \over 2}} \right]_0^2$$

$$ = \left[ {{1 \over 6}{{(2)}^3} - {{{{(2)}^2}} \over 2}} \right] - 0$$

$$ = {8 \over 6} - {4 \over 2} = {{8 - 12} \over 6}$$

$$ = - {4 \over 6} = {{ - 2} \over 3} = {2 \over 3}$$ sq. unit ($$\therefore$$ area will not be negative)

content: What is the area of the region bounded by | x | < 5, y = 0 and y = 8 ?
explanation:
Given curve y = 0 and y = 8 and | x | < 5

Case I When x < 0, then area of the region bounded

$$ = \int_{ - 5}^0 {0\,dx - \int_{ - 5}^0 {8\,dx = 0 - 8[x]_{ - 5}^0} } $$

$$ = - 8[0 + 5] = - 40$$

= 40 sq. units [$$\because$$ area will not be negative]

Case II When x > 0, then area of the region bounded

$$ = \int_0^5 {0\,dx - \int_0^5 {8\,dx = 0 - 8[x]_0^5} } $$

$$ = - 8[5 - 0] = - 40$$

= 40 sq. units

$$\therefore$$ Required area = 40 + 40 = 80 sq. units

Image

content: What is the area of  one of the loops between the curve y = c sin x and X-axis?
explanation:
$$\therefore$$ Required area = $$2\int_0^\pi {c\sin x\,dx} $$

Image

$$ = 2c\int_0^\pi {\sin x\,dx} $$

$$ = 2c[ - \cos x]_0^\pi $$

$$ = 2c[ - (\cos \pi - \cos 0)]$$

$$ = 2c(2)$$

$$ = 4c$$ sq. units

comprehension:
For the following three (03) items:
Consider the function f(x) = x|x|
content: What is the area bounded by the curve f(x) the x-axis and the lines x = - 2 and x = 1
explanation:
The required area is:

$ \left| \int_{-2}^{0} x^2 \, dx \right| + \int_{0}^{1} x^2 \, dx $

$ = -\left[ \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_{-2}^{0} + \left[ \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_{0}^{1} $

$ = \left[ 0 + \frac{8}{3} \right] + \left[ \frac{1}{3} - 0 \right] = \frac{8}{3} + \frac{1}{3} = \frac{9}{3} = 3 $

Therefore, the correct answer is Option 4.

comprehension:
Consider the following for the two (02) items that follow:
The slope of the tangent to the curve y = f(x) at (x, f(x) ) is 4 for every real number x and the curve passes through the origin.
content: . What is the area bounded by the curve, the x-axis and the line x = 4?
explanation:
1. The question tells us that the slope of the curve everywhere is 4. This means the derivative, or rate of change, of the function is 4.

    So, $ f'(x) = 4 $

    To find the original function, we find the antiderivative:

    $$f(x)=\int 4\,dx=4x + C$$

  2. We also know the curve goes through the point (0, 0). That means when $ x = 0 $, $ y = 0 $.

    Plugging in, $$0 = f(0) = 4\cdot 0 + C \implies C = 0$$

    So the function is simply:

    $$y = f(x) = 4x$$

  3. We want the area between the curve $ y = 4x $, the x-axis, and the lines $ x = 0 $ and $ x = 4 $.

    To find this area, set up the definite integral:

    $$\text{Area} = \int_{0}^{4} y\,dx = \int_{0}^{4} 4x\,dx$$

  4. Now let's solve the integral:

    $$\int 4x\,dx = 2x^2$$
    So, $$\text{Area} = [2x^2]_{0}^{4} = 2\cdot4^2 - 2\cdot0^2 = 2\cdot16 = 32$$

Answer: 32 square units (Option C).

comprehension:
Consider the following for the two (02) items that follow:
Let the curve f(x) = |x - 3|
content: What is the area bounded by the curve f(x) and y = 3?
explanation:
The graphs of

$$y=f(x)=|x-3|\quad\text{and}\quad y=3$$

intersect where

$$|x-3|=3\;\Rightarrow\;x-3=\pm3\;\Rightarrow\;x=0\text{ or }6.$$

So the bounded region is the “inverted-V” below the line $y=3$ and above the vertex at $(3,0)$, with endpoints $(0,3)$ and $(6,3)$.

• These three points $(0,3),\,(3,0),\,(6,3)$ form a triangle.

• Base of the triangle lies along $y=3$ from $x=0$ to $x=6$, so

$$\text{base}=6-0=6\text{ units.}$$

• Height is the vertical distance from the line $y=3$ down to the vertex $y=0$, so

$$\text{height}=3-0=3\text{ units.}$$

Area of a triangle is $\tfrac12\times\text{base}\times\text{height}$:

$$\text{Area}=\frac12\times6\times3=9\text{ square units.}$$

Answer: Option D (9 square units).

comprehension:
Consider the following for the two (02) items that follow :
Let the function f(x) = x2- 1
content: What is the area bounded by the function f(x) and the x-axis?
explanation:
The curve cuts the x–axis where

$$x^2-1=0\;\Longrightarrow\;x=\pm1.$$

On the interval $[-1,1]$, $f(x)=x^2-1\le0$, so the required area is

$ \int_{-1}^{1}\bigl|x^2-1\bigr|\,dx =\int_{-1}^{1}(1-x^2)\,dx. $

Step-by-step:

Write the integral:

$$A=\int_{-1}^{1}(1-x^2)\,dx.$$

Find an antiderivative:

$$\int(1-x^2)\,dx = x - \frac{x^3}{3} + C.$$

Evaluate from $-1$ to $1$:

$ A=\Bigl[x - \tfrac{x^3}{3}\Bigr]_{-1}^{1} =\Bigl(1-\tfrac{1}{3}\Bigr)-\Bigl(-1+\tfrac{1}{3}\Bigr) =\tfrac{2}{3}-\bigl(-\tfrac{2}{3}\bigr) =\tfrac{4}{3}. $

Hence the area is $\displaystyle \tfrac{4}{3}$ square units.

Answer: Option C.

comprehension:
Direction : Consider the following for the items that follow :

The area bounded by the parabola y2 = kx and the line x = k, where k > 0, is 4/3 square units.
content: What is the value of k?
explanation:
Explanation:

Given:

The area bounded by the parabola $ y^2 = kx $ and the line $ x = k $, where $ k > 0 $, is $ \frac{4}{3} $ square units.

The equation for the area bounded is:

$ \frac{4}{3} = 2 \int_0^k y \, dx $

Substituting $ y $ from the parabola equation $ y^2 = kx $, we have:

$ y = \sqrt{kx} $

So the integral becomes:

$ \frac{4}{3} = 2 \int_0^k \sqrt{k} \sqrt{x} \, dx $

This simplifies to:

$ 1 = k^2 $

Solving for $ k $, we find:

$ k = \pm 1 $

Since $ k > 0 $, the value of $ k $ is:

$ k = 1 $

Therefore, the value of $ k $ is 1.

comprehension:
Direction : Consider the following for the items that follow :

The area bounded by the parabola y2 = kx and the line x = k, where k > 0, is 4/3 square units.
content: What is the area of the parabola bounded by the latus rectum?
explanation:
Explanation:

Given:

The area bounded by the parabola $ y^2 = kx $ and the line $ x = k $, where $ k > 0 $, is $ \frac{4}{3} $ square units.

$ \text{Area bounded} = \frac{4}{3} \, \text{sq. units} $

$ = 2 \int_0^k y \, dx $

Given:

$ \frac{4}{3} = 2 \int_0^k \sqrt{k} \sqrt{x} \, dx $

Therefore, solving the equation:

$ 1 = k^2 $

Since $ k > 0 $, $ k = 1 $.

Thus, the equation of the parabola becomes $ y^2 = x $.

For the parabola, $ 4a = 1 $:

$ a = \frac{1}{4} $

The area of the parabola $ y^2 = x $ bounded by the latus rectum at $ x = \frac{1}{4} $ is:

$ = 2 \int_0^{\frac{1}{4}} \sqrt{x} \, dx $

$ = 2 \times \frac{2}{3} \times \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}} $

$ = \frac{1}{6} \, \text{sq. units} $

∴ The correct area is $\frac{1}{6}$ square units.

content: What is the area between the curve f(x) = x |x| and x-axis for x = [-1, 1]?
explanation:
To find the area between the curve $ f(x) = x |x| $ and the x-axis for $ x $ in the interval $[-1, 1]$, we need to evaluate the definite integral of $ |x| \cdot x $ from $-1$ to $1$:

$$ \int_{-1}^{1} f(x) \, dx = \int_{-1}^{1} x|x| \, dx $$

Breaking it down into two integrals over the intervals $[-1, 0]$ and $[0, 1]$:

For $x \in [-1, 0]$, $ x |x| = x^2 $ because $ |x| = -x $:

$$ \int_{-1}^{0} x|x| \, dx = \int_{-1}^{0} x^2 \, dx $$

For $x \in [0, 1]$, $ x |x| = x^2 $ because $ |x| = x $:

$$ \int_{0}^{1} x|x| \, dx = \int_{0}^{1} x^2 \, dx $$

Calculate each integral:

$$ \int_{-1}^{0} x^2 \, dx = \left[ \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_{-1}^{0} = \left( \frac{0^3}{3} - \frac{(-1)^3}{3} \right) = 0 - \left( -\frac{1}{3} \right) = \frac{1}{3} $$

$$ \int_{0}^{1} x^2 \, dx = \left[ \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_{0}^{1} = \left( \frac{1^3}{3} - \frac{0^3}{3} \right) = \frac{1}{3} - 0 = \frac{1}{3} $$

Add the results:

$$ \int_{-1}^{1} x|x| \, dx = \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{3} = \frac{2}{3} $$

Hence, the area between the curve $ f(x) = x |x| $ and the x-axis for $ x $ in the interval $[-1, 1]$ is:

$$ \boxed{\frac{2}{3}} $$

content: For what value of m with m < 0, is the area bounded by the lines y = x, y = mx and x = 2 equal to 3 ?
explanation:
To determine the value of $$m$$ for which the area bounded by the lines $$y = x$$, $$y = mx$$, and $$x = 2$$ is equal to 3, we need to find the area of the triangle formed by these lines. The vertices of the triangle are the points of intersection of these lines.

The lines intersect at the following points:

  • $$y = x$$ and $$x = 2$$ intersect at the point $$(2, 2)$$.
  • $$y = mx$$ and $$x = 2$$ intersect at the point $$(2, 2m)$$.
  • The lines $$y = x$$ and $$y = mx$$ intersect at the origin $$(0, 0)$$ because at this point, $$y = x = mx$$ implies $$x(1 - m) = 0$$, so $$x = 0$$.

The area of the triangle can be found using the formula for the area of a triangle with vertices at $$(x_1, y_1)$$, $$(x_2, y_2)$$, and $$(x_3, y_3)$$:

$$ \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \left| x_1(y_2 - y_3) + x_2(y_3 - y_1) + x_3(y_1 - y_2) \right| $$

Substituting the coordinates of the vertices $$(0, 0)$$, $$(2, 2)$$, and $$(2, 2m)$$:

$$ \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \left| 0(2 - 2m) + 2(2m - 0) + 2(0 - 2) \right| $$

Simplifying inside the absolute value:

$$ \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \left| 2(2m) + 2(-2) \right| $$

$$ = \frac{1}{2} \left| 4m - 4 \right| $$

Since we are given that the area is 3, we set up the equation:

$$ \frac{1}{2} \left| 4m - 4 \right| = 3 $$

Multiplying both sides by 2 to eliminate the fraction:

$$ \left| 4m - 4 \right| = 6 $$

This absolute value equation splits into two cases:

1. $$4m - 4 = 6$$

2. $$4m - 4 = -6$$

Solving the first case:

$$ 4m - 4 = 6 $$

$$ 4m = 10 $$

$$ m = \frac{5}{2} $$

Since this is positive, it does not satisfy $$m < 0$$.

Solving the second case:

$$ 4m - 4 = -6 $$

$$ 4m = -2 $$

$$ m = -\frac{1}{2} $$

This value is negative and satisfies $$m < 0$$.

Therefore, the correct value of $$m$$ is

$$ m = -\frac{1}{2} $$

The correct option is $$- \frac{1}{2}$$.

content: What is the area of the region (in the first quadrant) bounded by y = $\sqrt{1−\text{x}^2}$, y = x and y = 0 ?
explanation:
To find the area of the region in the first quadrant bounded by the curves $ y = \sqrt{1 - x^2} $, $ y = x $, and $ y = 0 $, we need to determine the points of intersection and set up the appropriate integral.

First, we find the points of intersection of the curves $ y = \sqrt{1 - x^2} $ and $ y = x $.

Set $ \sqrt{1 - x^2} = x $. Squaring both sides, we get:

$$ 1 - x^2 = x^2 $$

Solving for $ x $, we get:

$$ 1 = 2x^2 \implies x^2 = \frac{1}{2} \implies x = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} $$

Therefore, the curves intersect at $ \left( \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}, \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \right) $.

The desired region is from $ x = 0 $ to $ x = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} $. We divide the area into two parts:

  • The area under $ y = \sqrt{1 - x^2} $ from $ x = 0 $ to $ x = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} $.
  • The area under $ y = x $ from $ x = 0 $ to $ x = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} $.

The total area can be calculated as the difference of these two integrals:

The area under $ y = \sqrt{1 - x^2} $ from $ x = 0 $ to $ x = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} $:

$$ \text{Area}_1 = \int_0^{\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}} \sqrt{1 - x^2} \, dx $$

The area under $ y = x $ from $ x = 0 $ to $ x = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} $:

$$ \text{Area}_2 = \int_0^{\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}} x \, dx $$

Using the integral method, we find:

For $ \text{Area}_1 $, we use the substitution $ x = \sin \theta $, $ dx = \cos \theta \, d\theta $, and the limits change from $ x=0 $ to $ x=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} $ which means $ \theta $ ranges from $ 0 $ to $ \frac{\pi}{4} $:

$$ \int_0^{\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}} \sqrt{1 - x^2} \, dx = \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \cos^2 \theta \, d\theta $$

Using the identity $ \cos^2 \theta = \frac{1 + \cos 2\theta}{2} $, the integral becomes:

$$ \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \cos^2 \theta \, d\theta = \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{1 + \cos 2\theta}{2} \, d\theta $$

Evaluating this integral, we get:

$$ \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{1 + \cos 2\theta}{2} \, d\theta = \left[ \frac{\theta}{2} + \frac{\sin 2\theta}{4} \right]_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} = \frac{\pi}{8} + \frac{0}{4} = \frac{\pi}{8} $$

Similarly, calculating $ \text{Area}_2 $:

$$ \int_0^{\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}} x \, dx = \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} \right]_0^{\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}} = \frac{ \left( \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \right)^2 }{2} = \frac{1}{4} $$

The area of the region bounded by the curves in the first quadrant is:

$$ \text{Area} = \text{Area}_1 - \text{Area}_2 = \frac{\pi}{8} - \frac{1}{4} = \frac{\pi}{8} - \frac{2}{8} = \frac{\pi - 2}{8} $$

Thus, the appropriate multiple choice option that matches this result is:

Option C: $ \frac{\pi}{8} $

content: What is the area of the region bounded by x − |y| = 0 and x − 2 = 0 ?
explanation:
To determine the area of the region bounded by the lines $ x - |y| = 0 $ and $ x - 2 = 0 $, we'll first analyze these equations and then find the enclosed area.

The equation $ x - |y| = 0 $ can be written as:

$$ x = |y| $$

This equation represents two lines, $ x = y $ and $ x = -y $. These lines form a 'V' shape on the coordinate plane, with the vertex at the origin $(0,0)$.

The equation $ x - 2 = 0 $ simply represents the vertical line:

$$ x = 2 $$

Let's visualize these lines on a coordinate plane:

  • Line 1: $ x = y $
  • Line 2: $ x = -y $
  • Line 3: $ x = 2 $

The region of interest is bounded by these lines, forming a triangle between the origin $(0,0)$, the point where $ x = y $ intersects $ x = 2 $ (i.e., $(2,2)$), and the point where $ x = -y $ intersects $ x = 2 $ (i.e., $(2,-2)$).

To find the area of this triangular region, we use the formula for the area of a triangle:

$$ \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \text{base} \cdot \text{height} $$

The base of the triangle is the vertical distance between the points $(2,2)$ and $ (2,-2) $, which is:

$$ 2 - (-2) = 4 $$

The height is the horizontal distance from the origin $(0,0)$ to the line $ x = 2 $, which is:

$$ 2 $$

Therefore, the area is:

$$ \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 4 \cdot 2 = 4 $$

Thus, the area of the region bounded by the given lines is:

Option C: 4

content: What is the area of the region enclosed in the first quadrant by x2 + y2 = π2, y = sin x and x = 0 ?
explanation:
To find the area of the region enclosed in the first quadrant by the given curves $x^2 + y^2 = \pi^2$, $y = \sin x$, and $x = 0$, we need to take the following steps:

  1. Determine the limits of integration
  2. Calculate the area under $y = \sin x$ from $x=0$ to $x=\pi$
  3. Calculate the area of the circle segment from $x=0$ to $x=\pi$
  4. Subtract the two areas to find the enclosed region

First, observe that $x^2 + y^2 = \pi^2$ is a circle centered at the origin with radius $\pi$. In the first quadrant, the limits of integration for $x$ range from 0 to $\pi$.

We start by calculating the area under the curve $y = \sin x$ from $x = 0$ to $x = \pi$. The integral for this area $A_{\text{sin}}$ is:

$$ A_{\text{sin}} = \int_0^{\pi} \sin x \, dx $$

Solving this integral, we have:

$$ A_{\text{sin}} = [-\cos x]_0^{\pi} = -\cos(\pi) - (-\cos(0)) = -(-1) - (-1) = 2 $$

Next, we calculate the area of the quarter circle from $x=0$ to $x=\pi$ in the first quadrant. The area $A_{\text{circle}}$ is:

$$ A_{\text{circle}} = \frac{1}{4} \times \pi \times (\pi^2) = \frac{\pi^3}{4} $$

Therefore, to find the desired area, we subtract the area under $y = \sin x$ from the area of the quarter circle:

$$ A_{\text{enclosed}} = A_{\text{circle}} - A_{\text{sin}} = \frac{\pi^3}{4} - 2 $$

Thus, the correct answer is:

Option B $$\frac{\pi^3}{4} - 2$$

content: What is the area included in the first quadrant between the curves y = x and y = x<sup>3 </sup>?
explanation:
To determine the area enclosed in the first quadrant between the curves $ y = x $ and $ y = x^3 $, we first need to identify the points of intersection of these curves. This requires solving the equation:

$$x = x^3$$

Simplifying, we get:

$$x^3 - x = 0$$

Factoring, we have:

$$x(x^2 - 1) = 0$$

Further factorizing, we get:

$$x(x - 1)(x + 1) = 0$$

This equation gives us three solutions: $ x = 0 $, $ x = 1 $, and $ x = -1 $. Since we're only interested in the first quadrant, we focus on $ x = 0 $ and $ x = 1 $.

Next, to find the area between these curves from $ x = 0 $ to $ x = 1 $, we set up the integral:

$$\text{Area} = \int_{0}^{1} (y_{\text{upper}} - y_{\text{lower}}) \, dx$$

Here, $ y_{\text{upper}} = x $ and $ y_{\text{lower}} = x^3 $. Hence, the integral becomes:

$$\text{Area} = \int_{0}^{1} (x - x^3) \, dx$$

We now evaluate this integral step by step:

First, integrate:

$$\int_{0}^{1} x \, dx - \int_{0}^{1} x^3 \, dx$$

For the first term:

$$\int x \, dx = \frac{x^2}{2}$$

For the second term:

$$\int x^3 \, dx = \frac{x^4}{4}$$

Combining these and evaluating from 0 to 1, we have:

$$\left[ \frac{x^2}{2} - \frac{x^4}{4} \right]_{0}^{1}$$

Substituting the limits:

$$\left( \frac{1^2}{2} - \frac{1^4}{4} \right) - \left( \frac{0^2}{2} - \frac{0^4}{4} \right)$$

This simplifies to:

$$\left( \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{4} \right) - 0 = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{4} = \frac{2}{4} - \frac{1}{4} = \frac{1}{4}$$

Hence, the area included in the first quadrant between the curves $ y = x $ and $ y = x^3 $ is:

$$\frac{1}{4} \ \text{square unit}$$

So, the correct answer is Option B:

$$\frac{1}{4} \ \text{square unit}$$

content: What is the area bounded by y = [x], where [⋅] is the greatest integer function, the x-axis and the lines x = -1.5 and x = -1.8?
explanation:
To solve this problem, let's break it down step by step:

Identify the function:

The function given is the greatest integer (floor) function, $$y = [x].$$ This function returns the largest integer less than or equal to $$x.$$

Determine the value of the function on the interval:

We are considering $$x$$ values between -1.8 and -1.5. For any $$x$$ in this range:

Since $$-2 < x < -1,$$ the greatest integer less than or equal to $$x$$ is $$-2.$$

Therefore, in the interval $$x = -1.8$$ to $$x = -1.5,$$ we have:

$$y = -2.$$

Find the width of the region:

The region is bounded by the vertical lines at $$x = -1.5$$ and $$x = -1.8.$$

The width is:

$$\Delta x = |-1.5 - (-1.8)| = 0.3.$$

Determine the height of the region:

The area is bounded by the graph $$y = -2$$ and the x-axis (which is $$y = 0$$).

The vertical distance between these is:

$$\Delta y = |0 - (-2)| = 2.$$

Calculate the area:

Since the region is a rectangle, the area $$A$$ is given by:

$$A = \Delta x \times \Delta y = 0.3 \times 2 = 0.6 \text{ square units}.$$

Thus, the area bounded by $$y = [x],$$ the x-axis, and the lines $$x = -1.5$$ and $$x = -1.8$$ is $$0.6$$ square units.

The correct option is Option C.

content: What is the area bounded by $\rm y=\sqrt{16-x^2}$, y ≥ 0 and the x-axis?
explanation:
Concept:

To find the area under the function $ y = f(x) $ from $ x = a $ to $ x = b $, use the definite integral $\left|\int_a^b f(x)\, dx\right|$ for curves that are entirely on one side of the x-axis.

If the curve extends on both sides of the x-axis, calculate the areas for each segment separately and add them.

Definite integral: If $\int f(x)\, dx = g(x) + C$, then $\int_a^b f(x)\, dx = [g(x)]_a^b = g(b) - g(a)$.

The integral of a semi-circle or part of a circle: $\int \sqrt{a^2-x^2}\, dx = \frac{x}{2}\sqrt{a^2-x^2} + \frac{a^2}{2}\sin^{-1} \frac{x}{a} + C$.

Calculation:

To determine where the curve meets the x-axis (i.e., where $ y = 0 $):

$ \sqrt{16-x^2} = 0 $

$ \Rightarrow x = \pm 4 $

Since the curve $ y = \sqrt{16-x^2} $ lies entirely above the x-axis for $ x = -4 $ to $ x = 4 $:

The area is given by:

$ \int_{-4}^{4} \sqrt{16 - x^2}\, dx $

Evaluating this integral, we have:

$ \left[\frac{x}{2}\sqrt{16-x^2} + \frac{16}{2}\sin^{-1}\frac{x}{4}\right]_{-4}^{4} $

Substituting the limits:

$ = \left[\frac{4}{2}\sqrt{16-16} + \frac{16}{2}\sin^{-1}\frac{4}{4}\right] - \left[\frac{-4}{2}\sqrt{16-16} + \frac{16}{2}\sin^{-1}\frac{-4}{4}\right] $

Simplifying:

$ = 8 \frac{\pi}{2} + 8 \frac{\pi}{2} $

Therefore, the area is:

$ \textbf{8π square units}. $
